Author Headline: Founder’s Office @ SackBerry | LinkedIn Growth & Personal Branding for Founders & CXOs
Description:
I used to think paid ads were the safest bet.

You control the budget, track results in real time, and everything feels predictable.

But the numbers say something interesting.

Paid search ads return roughly ₹400–₹500 for every ₹100 spent.
Display ads are closer to ₹200 per ₹100.

Influencer marketing averages around ₹600 per ₹100 invested, with top campaigns reaching ₹1800–₹2000 per ₹100.

The trust factor is the real difference.

About 70% of consumers trust influencer recommendations more than brand ads, and most brands now prefer nano and micro influencers over big celebrities.

Smaller audience.
Higher trust.
Better conversions.

It’s not really influencers vs ads anymore.

The real opportunity is using both together.

Is influencer marketing still an experiment for you, or already a core channel?

#InfluencerMarketing #DigitalMarketing #GrowthMarketing

Author Headline: CMO @ AJ Marketing | Creative marketing inspired by K-celebrities, creators & artists 🚀
Description:
Influencer marketing is quietly changing in 2026.

For years, the model was simple.

Brand pays.
Creator posts.
Campaign ends.

But the more campaigns we run across APAC, the more obvious the shift becomes:
→ The pay-to-post model is slowly dying.
→ One-off posts rarely move the needle anymore.

Why?
Because audiences don’t build trust in a single post.
And algorithms don’t reward one-time brand mentions either.

What we see working now looks very different.
Brands are building long-term creator relationships, not short campaigns.

They tell real stories.
They integrate the product naturally into their content.

Over time, the audience stops seeing it as an ad,
but as part of the creator’s life.

And that’s when influence actually starts to work.

In 2026, influencer marketing isn’t about buying reach.
It’s about building creator ecosystems.

Author Headline: Meta & Google Ads | On-Page SEO | Local SEO | Performance Marketing & ROAS Growth
Description:
Influencer marketing for a local restaurant with £500 budget? 🍔
Please don't. Do this instead.

I see it every week.

Restaurant owner pays a "food influencer" £500.
Gets 10,000 views on Instagram.
Gets 3 walk-ins.
Wonders why it didn't work. 🤦

Here's the hard truth:

Influencer views don't pay your rent.
Customers do. 🏠

With that same £500 here's what actually works:

✅ Meta Ads targeting people within 3 miles of your restaurant
→ Direct bookings. Measurable results.

✅ Google Ads targeting "restaurants near me" searches
→ High intent. People already hungry and looking.

✅ GMB posts with a weekly offer
→ Free. Drives walk-ins from local searches.

Influencers build awareness.
Ads build bookings.

If you have £500 and you need customers THIS week
put it in targeted ads. Not followers.

Agree or disagree? 💬 Comment below 👇

#RestaurantMarketing #InfluencerMarketing #MetaAds #FoodBusiness #LocalMarketing

Author Headline: Influencer Marketing | Content Creator | Creative & Strategic Professional
Description:
After working on multiple influencer campaigns, I’ve realized something,

Influencer marketing is broken.
Not because creators aren’t talented.
Not because audiences don’t trust influencers.
It’s broken because most campaigns are built on the wrong priorities.

Brands chase big follower counts.
Agencies chase quick reach.
Creators chase paid collaborations.

But somewhere in between, the actual audience gets ignored.
And audiences are smarter than we think.

They can instantly tell when a promotion is forced, scripted, or purely transactional.
The campaigns that actually work?
They don’t feel like ads.
They feel like recommendations.

Maybe influencer marketing doesn’t need more budgets.
Maybe it just needs better creator brand fit, honesty, and real storytelling.

Just a thought.

Author Headline: Influencer Marketing | Campaign Strategy | Creator Partnerships Helping Brands Scale with Performance-Driven Campaigns
Description:
Marketers,
One question I hear all the time about influencer marketing is:
“How much budget do you actually need for a campaign?”
The reality is that influencer campaigns can scale depending on your goals.
Here’s a simple way many brands structure their budgets:
🔹 Nano creators (1K–10K followers)
Great for authenticity and niche communities.
Typical cost: ₹2K–₹10K per post.
🔹 Micro creators (10K–100K followers)
Strong engagement and trusted audiences.
Typical cost: ₹10K–₹60K per post.
🔹 Macro creators (100K+)
Large reach and strong brand awareness.
Costs can go from ₹60K to ₹3L+ per collaboration.
Many brands today prioritize nano & micro influencers because they often deliver better engagement and ROI compared to large celebrity creators. (Hobo.Video)
A smart campaign budget usually covers:
• Creator fees
• Content production (Reels, videos, posts)
• Paid amplification
• Campaign management & reporting
Influencer marketing isn’t just about reach anymore it’s about community trust, authentic storytelling, and measurable impact.
Curious to know from other marketers here:
👉 How do you structure your influencer campaign budgets in 2026?
#InfluencerMarketing #CreatorEconomy #MarketingStrategy #DigitalMarketing #BrandBuilding

Author Headline: 8,883 followers
Description:
Influencer marketing has come a long way… but the misconceptions around it haven’t 🥲

We still hear the same ones pop up in conversations with brands 👇

“Big following = big results.”
Not always. An influencer with 50k engaged followers can outperform someone with 500k if the audience is the right fit. Relevance > reach.

“Influencer marketing is just posting a photo.”
The best campaigns are much more than that. Strategy, storytelling, creative direction, distribution and timing all matter just as much as the post itself.

“It’s impossible to measure.”
It’s actually very measurable when done properly. From trackable links and codes to brand lift and content performance, there are plenty of ways to understand impact.

“One post will change everything.”
Like most marketing channels, consistency wins. The brands seeing the best results treat influencer as a long-term channel, not a one-off test.
Influencer marketing works best when brands treat creators like creative partners, not just media space.

What influencer misconceptions do you hear the most? 🤔

Author Headline: Growth Architect | Creator-led Acquisition | Partnerships & Community Engines | Ex- BoxyCharm | Advisor to Founders & PE-Backed Consumer Brands
Description:
Most companies hire one influencer marketing manager…

…and expect them to run two completely different strategies.

Over the years I’ve noticed something interesting.

Influencer marketing typically sits in one of two lanes inside a company and while both are incredibly valuable, they operate very differently.

Growth-Focused Influencer Marketing
Primary objective: conversion & measurable revenue
• Affiliate partnerships
• Discount codes / trackable links
• Creator-led performance ads
• Optimized for CAC, ROAS, and contribution margin
This lane is performance-heavy and highly analytical.
Success is measured directly against revenue outcomes.

Brand-Focused Influencer Marketing
Primary objective: awareness, affinity, and cultural relevance
• Story-driven content
• UGC creation
• Trend-led collaborations
• Creator partnerships that fuel brand narrative
This lane builds perception, community, and brand equity which often drives performance indirectly over time.
Both are essential.

But they require different strategy, different measurement frameworks, and often times different operators.

I saw this play out firsthand while helping scale the creator ecosystem at BoxyCharm (IPSY).

Even back in 2017, it was clear that brand and growth couldn’t operate in silos if influencer was going to become a real growth engine for the business.

So we built infrastructure that intentionally connected the two:
Brand storytelling + creator content paired with performance distribution and measurable acquisition.

When those pieces work together, influencer stops being “a marketing channel” and becomes a business driver.

Curious how other teams are structuring this today:
Does influencer sit under
Brand
Growth
or both?

Description:
Influencer marketing isn’t one-size-fits-all — and that’s actually good news for your brand.

Sponsored posts get a lot of attention, but they’re just one tool in the toolbox. If they’re not driving results for you, that doesn’t mean influencer marketing has failed you. It means you haven’t found your version of it yet.

The brands seeing the biggest returns aren’t necessarily the ones with the biggest partnerships — they’re the ones who’ve identified the format, creator type, and content style that aligns authentically with their brand identity. That might look like long-form reviews, UGC-style content, niche micro-influencers, or something else entirely.

The strategy that works is the one built around your brand — not someone else’s playbook.

What’s an influencer marketing approach you’ve seen work (or not work) for a brand? Drop it in the comments. ↓

Author Headline: Campaign specialist at Anthill media
Description:
Last week a brand asked me something interesting.
“Can you just get us 50 influencers to post about our product?”
I asked them one question.
Do you want influencers… or do you want results?
Because influencer marketing isn’t about how many creators post.
It’s about how the campaign is built.
Here’s what actually happens behind every successful influencer campaign 👇
1️⃣ Strategy
Before reaching out to creators, we define:
• campaign objective
• target audience
• platform strategy
• content direction
Without this, it’s just random posting.
2️⃣ Creator Selection
Follower count isn’t the real metric.
We look at:
• audience relevance
• engagement patterns
• content style
• brand fit
Sometimes 10 right creators outperform 100 random ones.
3️⃣ Content & Storytelling
People scroll past ads.
But they stop for stories.
So the goal is to create content that feels native to the platform, not forced promotion.
4️⃣ Execution
A campaign with 20 creators can involve:
• multiple briefs
• approvals
• revisions
• posting timelines
• coordination across teams
There’s a lot more happening than just “posting”.
5️⃣ Performance Tracking
The real metrics are:
• watch time
• saves
• shares
• meaningful engagement
• conversions
Because reach alone doesn’t build brands.
Influencer marketing works incredibly well.
But only when it’s treated as a strategy not just a transaction.
Curious
What’s the biggest mistake you’ve seen brands make with influencer marketing?
#InfluencerMarketing #CreatorEconomy #MarketingStrategy #LinkedInMarketing #DigitalMarketing

Author Headline: 9+ Years in Influencer Marketing for big brands, agencies and scrappy start-ups
Description:
In my influencer marketing lifetime, I’ve probably looked at hundreds of thousands of creator profiles. 

If I were to give CREATORS 3 quick tips that go a LONG way in getting you hired, they’d be this:

1. PLEASE… PLEASE put your email in your bio.
 Brands and agencies want to reach you quickly. Don’t make us dig.

2. If you’re sending a cold pitch, include hyperlinks to your social profiles.
 Make it easy for us to see your content. Bonus points if you link to specific posts you’re proud of.

Honestly, I prefer this to a generic media kit, most influencer tools already tell me what your media kit does.

3. Pitch ideas.
What would an ideal partnership look like to you? Paint a quick picture of how you’d naturally integrate the brand into your content.

What would you add?

Description:
Uncomfortable Wellness Truth #1: Influencer marketing is borrowed trust & it expires.

A creator can drive traffic. Great! 

But what they can’t do is this: Carry your credibility long-term.

If conversions spike only when someone else speaks for you, that’s not growth.

That’s dependency. 

Do you really want to be dependent on influencers? 

First things first: A great product that solves your audience’s problem.  

Founders should ask:

If every influencer stopped posting tomorrow, will my brand still feel authoritative and trustworthy?

Build owned credibility.
Then amplify it.

Author Headline: Founder: Creative Social Media Agency
Description:
One thing I see brands waste a lot of money on is influencer marketing done the lazy way: A creator has a big following, asks for $3K+ for a post, and brands assume it’s a good deal.

But follower count alone means nothing anymore. There are a lot of creators with inflated audiences, hidden like counts, and the same circle of friends commenting on every post to make engagement look higher than it really is. It’s called fake engagement and unfortunately a lot of brands are still falling for it.

A big following doesn’t impress me anymore. Show me the data!
When I work with creators, I ask for their social metrics from the last three months. Reach. Saves. Shares. Audience breakdown. I want to see what’s actually happening behind the posts.

I’m also looking at things most people miss. Are the comments real? Does the audience match the brand? Does the creator actually influence their community or are they just posting content?

And beyond numbers, I care about alignment. The best partnerships are with creators who genuinely fit the brand and would talk about the product even if they weren’t paid.

Influencer marketing can work incredibly well when it’s done right. But when it’s done lazily, it burns budget fast.

This is where experience matters. I’ve been doing this long enough to spot the red flags pretty quickly 🤓 🔍 📊

Author Headline: Influencer & Retention Marketing Specialist | Growth & ROI
Description:
Influencer marketing has changed a lot over the years.

Earlier, it was mostly about finding creators with a huge following, paying them for a post, and hoping the numbers looked good. But today, it’s much more than that.

From my experience working in influencer marketing, the biggest shift I’ve seen is that people care more about authenticity now.

Brands are no longer just looking at follower counts. They want creators who have a real connection with their audience. Someone whose followers actually listen, trust their opinions, and engage with their content.

Another big change is moving away from one-time collaborations. Long-term partnerships between brands and creators are becoming more common because they feel more genuine to the audience.

Also, audiences today can instantly tell when something feels forced. The campaigns that perform the best are the ones where creators talk about a product in their own style and voice, instead of sounding like an advertisement.

Working on the brand side has taught me one important thing: the best influencer campaigns happen when brands trust creators to be themselves.

Influencer marketing isn’t just about promotion anymore.It’s about building real connections with the audience.

Curious to know if you work in marketing or the creator space, what changes have you noticed in influencer marketing?

Author Headline: Helping Web3, AI & FinTech brands scale via influencer marketing
Description:
Why do some influencers walk away from money?

A strange situation happened to me recently.

For almost a month I tried to reach one influencer.
Eventually he agreed to collaborate.

We structured the deal simply:
50% upfront, 50% after publishing.

I sent the first payment.

The video was recorded.
The product approved the content.

All he had to do was press “publish.”

The second payment waiting for him was ~$3,000.

And then… he disappeared.

No replies.
No reads.
No publication.

Completely gone.

At first I assumed something serious happened.

Then I considered another possibility:
maybe he signed an exclusive contract.
Or intentionally stalled the campaign.

But the reality is simple — I never heard from him again.

Lesson learned.

Today my rule is straightforward:

• If an influencer replies slowly — I’m cautious.
• If reliability is unclear — I investigate.
• If something feels off — I check with agencies or colleagues who worked with them before.

Influencer marketing is not only about reach.
It’s about trust and operational discipline.

Because sometimes people literally walk away from money.

Curious to hear from others in the industry.

What’s the strangest thing that happened to you when working with influencers?

Drop your stories in the comments 👇

Author Headline: Social Media & Influencer Marketing Strategist | Driving influence through social, creators, and employee advocacy
Description:
“Who has the biggest audience?” is still the default influencer marketing question.

It’s also one of the least useful now.

Follower count won't tell you:
→ how many people will actually see the post
→ whether the audience is the right one
→ whether the creator can move prospects through the pipeline

What matters more:
→ audience relevance (do they reach the people you want to reach?)
→ consistency (can they repeat a message that naturally fits their brand?)
→ proof of outcomes (clicks, sign-ups, comments from prospects)

If you’re shortlisting creators, start with who consistently reaches the right people and can drive the outcome you care about. The rest is just a number.

When was the last time a smaller creator outperformed a bigger one for you?

Author Headline: Influencer Marketing | Brand Partnerships & Media | Driving Growth Through SaaS & Strategic Partnerships
Description:
Petition to move influencer marketing toward performance-based pricing❗ 

Because here's the reality: A $10K post that delays a campaign by 3 months… isn’t entirely worth $10K.

Flat rate pricing is helpful. It makes budgeting simple and negotiations straightforward. But the contract price is just the tip of the iceberg. 

Rates should not be based on follower count and vanity metrics.

It should also consider the creator whose performance includes:
•  Responsiveness
•  Following the brief
•  Minimal revisions
•  On-time delivery
•  Ad-friendly content quality

In my experience, the creators and talent managers I'll be reaching out to again are the ones whose performance and price worked together throughout the entire process.

Because great creators don’t just deliver posts.
They deliver reliability, collaboration, and content that performs long after it’s published.

Campaign managers — what’s your take on this?
